Primary Reason for Home Health/Medical Necessity (POC Element):
The patient is considered homebound because he has severe left knee medial compartment osteoarthritis with a flexible 5° varus deformity and medial joint-line tenderness that produces sharp
pain with activity and aching at rest, and he cannot ambulate more than 200 meters at a time due to knee pain. Exam findings document limited functional mobility and pain limiting therapeutic
exercise tolerance; the patient reports difficulty ascending and descending stairs and requires assistive-device training and home safety/fall-precaution interventions (physical therapy was
ordered for gait/balance/transfer training, assistive device education, ROM and therapeutic strengthening, and post-op exercise preparation). Leaving the home requires a considerable and
taxing effort because even short bouts of ambulation provoke severe pain, impair safe stair negotiation, and necessitate assistance and optimization of transfers and assistive-device use.
Allowable absences should be limited and infrequent restricted to medically necessary appointments or essential, short supervised trips only. These objective, documented functional
impairments and planned skilled interventions (PT for gait/transfer training, therapeutic exercise, assistive-device education, and home-safety/fall precautions) demonstrate that the patient
meets Medicare homebound criteria and that skilled home health services are medically necessary to enable safe mobility, prevent falls, and prepare for the planned partial knee replacement.
Criteria One (One must be met): Describe what assistive devices the patient is dependent on (if any) and specifically why the patient condition warrants the special transportation or the assistance
of another person or describe the condition that makes leaving home medically contraindicated.
Criteria 1 A: Because of illness or injury
Patient needs the aid of supportive devices
Patient Needs the use of special transportation.
Patient needs the assistance of another person to leave the place of residence
Criteria 1 B: Has a condition such as leaving his or her home is medically contraindicated.
Criteria 2 A: There must be exist a normal inability to leave home.
Unable to tolerate standing or walking for extended periods
Weakness and pain from recent surgery
Balance impaired with potential for falls; uses walls and furniture to ambulate
Cognitively impaired, unable to safely leave home without assist
Leaving home is exhausting, extended rest periods to recover
Infectious disease process causing weakness and exhaustion
Narcotics impaired ability to safely leave home unassisted
Requires human assist due to unsteady gait/poor balance
Criteria 2 B: Leaving home must require a considerable and taxing effort
Exhausting to navigate vehicle and require human assist
Leaving familiar environment causes fear and confusion
Requires assist to manage equipment and public transportation
Leaving home is strenous requiring extended rest to recover
Needs assistance to handle and transport equipment
Activities restricted due to pain limitations

List each diagnosis for which the patient is receiving home care in Column 1, and enter its ICD-10-CM code at the level of highest specificity in Column 2 (diagnosis codes only - no surgical or
procedure codes allowed). Diagnoses are listed in the order that best reflects the seriousness of each condition and supports the disciplines and services provided. Rate the degree of symptom
control for each condition in Column 2. ICD-10-CM sequencing requirements must be followed if multiple coding is indicated for any diagnoses.
Code each row according to the following directions for each column:
Column 1: Enter the description of the diagnosis. Sequencing of diagnoses should reflect the seriousness of each condition and support the disciplines and services provided. Column 2: Enter
the ICD-10-CM code for the condition described in Column 1- no surgical or procedure codes allowed. Codes must be entered at the level of highest specificity and ICD-10-CM coding rules and
sequencing requirements must be followed. Note that external cause codes (ICD-10-CM codes beginning with V, W, X, or Y) may not be reported in M1021 (Primary Diagnosis) but may be
reported in M1023 (Secondary Diagnoses). Also note that when a Z-code is reported in
Column 2 the code for the underlying condition can often be entered in Column 2, as long as it is an active on-going condition impacting home health care. Rate the degree of symptom control
for the condition listed in Column 1. Do not assign a symptom control rating if the diagnosis code is a V, W, X, Y or Z-code. Choose one value that represents the degree of symptom control
appropriate for each diagnosis using the following scale: 0 Asymptomatic, no treatment needed at this time Symptoms well controlled with current therapy 2- Symptoms controlled with
difficulty, affecting daily functioning; patient needs ongoing monitoring 3- Symptoms poorly controlled; patient needs frequent adjustment in treatment and dose monitoring 4 - Symptoms poorly
controlled; history of re-hospitalizations Note that the rating for symptom control in Column 2 should not be used to determine the sequencing of the diagnoses listed in Column 1. These are
separate items and sequencing may not coincide.
Z96.652 Presence of left artificial knee joint

Patient's current ability to prepare and take all oral medications reliably and safely, including administration of the correct dosage at the appropriate times/intervals. Excludes injectable and IV medicat
ions. (NOTE: This refers to ability, not compliance or willingness.)
0. Able to independently take the correct oral medication(s) and proper dosage(s) at the correct times.
1. Able to take medication(s) at the correct times if: (a) individual dosages are prepared in advance by another person; OR (b) another person develops a drug diary or chart.
2. Able to take medication(s) at the correct times if given reminders by another person at the appropriate times.
3. Unable to take medication unless administered by another person

Patient's current ability to prepare and take all prescribed injectable medications reliably and safely, including administration of correct dosage at the appropriate times/intervals. Excludes IV medicatio
0. Able to independently take the correct medication(s) and proper dosage(s) at the correct times
1. Able to take injectable medication(s) at the correct times if: a. individual syringes are prepared in advance by another person; OR b. another person develops a drug diary or
2. Able to take medication(s) at the correct times if given reminders by another person based on the frequency of the injection
3. Unable to take injectable medication unless administered by another person

Ask patient: "I am going to say three words for you to remember. Please repeat the words after I have said all three. The words are: SOCK, BLUE, and BED . Now
Number of words repeated after first attempt
After the patient's first attempt, repeat the words using cues ( "sock, something to wear; blue, a color; bed, a piece of furniture"). You may repeat the words up to two more times.

(M1740) Cognitive, behavioral, and psychiatric symptoms that are demonstrated at least once a week (Reported or Observed):
1. Memory deficit: failure to recognize familiar persons/places, inability to recall events of past 24 hours, significant memory loss so that supervision is required
2. Impaired decision-making: failure to perform usual ADLs or IADLs, inability to appropriately stop activities, jeopardizes safety through actions
3. Verbal disruption: yelling, threatening, excessive profanity, sexual references, etc.
4. Physical aggression: aggressive or combative to self and others (for example, hits self, throws objects, punches, dangerous maneuvers with wheelchair or other objects)
5. Disruptive, infantile, or socially inappropriate behavior (excludes verbal actions)
7. None of the above behaviors demonstrated
(M1745) Frequency of Disruptive Behavior Symptoms (Reported or Observed):

2. Requires assistance and some direction in specific situations (for example, on all tasks involving shifting of attention) or consistently requires low stimulus environment due
3. Requires considerable assistance in routine situations. Is not alert and oriented or is unable to shift attention and recall directions more than half the time.
4. Totally dependent due to disturbances such as constant disorientation, coma, persistent vegetative state, or delirium.

Ability to see in adequate light (with glasses or other visual appliances)
0. Adequate - sees fine detail, such as regular print in newspapers/books
1. Impaired - sees large print, but not regular print in newspapers/books
2. Moderate impaired - limited vision; not able to see newspaper headlines but can identify objects
3. Highly impaired - object identification in question, but eyes appear to follow objects
4. Severely impaired - no vision or sees only light, colors or shapes; eyes do not appear to follow objects
Ability to hear (with hearing aid or hearing appliances if normally used)
0. Adequate - no difficulty in normal conversation, social interaction, listening to TV
1. Minimal difficulty - difficulty in some environments (e.g., when person speaks softly, or setting is noisy)
2. Moderate difficulty - speaker has to increase volume and speak distinctly
3. Highly impaired - absence of useful hearing
(M1400) When is the patient dyspneic or noticeably Short of Breath?
0. Patient is not short of breath
1. When walking more than 20 feet, climbing stairs
2. With moderate exertion (for example, while dressing, using commode or bedpan, walking distances less than 20 feet)
3. With minimal exertion (for example, while eating, talking, or performing other ADLs) or with agitation
Current ability to feed self meals and snacks safely. Note: This refers only to the process of eating, chewing, and swallowing, not preparing the food to be eaten.
1. Able to feed self independently but requires: (a) meal set-up; OR (b) intermittent assistance or supervision from another person; OR (c) a liquid, pureed or ground meat diet.
2. Unable to feed self and must be assisted or supervised throughout the meal/snack.
3. Able to take in nutrients orally and receives supplemental nutrients though a nasogastric tube or gastrostomy.
4. Unable to take in nutrients orally and is fed nutrients through a nasogastric tube or gastrostomy.
5. Unable to take in nutrients orally or by tube feeding.

C. Mechanically altered diet - require change in texture of food or liquids (e.g., pureed food, thickened liquids)

(M1306) Does this patient have at least one Unhealed Pressure Ulcer/Injury at Stage 2 or Higher or designated as Unstageable?
(Excludes Stage 1 pressure injuries and all healed pressure ulcers/injuries)

Ulcer/Injury that is Stageable at DC]
(M1311) Current Number of Unhealed Pressure Ulcers/Injuries at Each Stage:
A1. Stage 2: Partial thickness loss of dermis presenting as a shallow open ulcer with a red or pink wound bed, without slough. May also present as an intact
or open/ruptured blister. Number of Stage 2 pressure ulcers
B1. Stage 3: Full thickness tissue loss. Subcutaneous fat may be visible but bone, tendon, or muscle is not exposed. Slough may be present but does not
obscure the depth of tissue loss. May include undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 3 pressure ulcers
C1. Stage 4: Full thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle. Slough or eschar may be present on some parts of the wound bed. Often
includes undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 4 pressure ulcers
D1. Unstageable: Non-removable dressing/device: Known but not stageable due to non- removable dressing/device Number of unstageable pressure
E1. Unstageable: Slough and/or eschar: Known but not stageable due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar Number of unstageable
pressure ulcers due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar
F1. Unstageable: Deep tissue injury Number of unstageable pressure injuries presenting as deep tissue injury
(M1322) Current Number of Stage 1 Pressure Injuries:
Intact skin with non blanchable redness of a localized area usually over a bony prominence. Darkly pigmented skin may not have a visible blanching; in dark skin tones only it may appear with persis
(M1324) Stage of Most Problematic Unhealed Pressure Ulcer/Injury that is Stageable:
(Excludes pressure ulcer/injury that cannot be staged due to a non removable dressing/device, coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar, or deep tissue injury.)
Stage of Most Problematic: Patient has no pressure ulcers or no stageable pressure ulcers

Which of the following signs or symptoms characterize this patient as at risk for hospitalization? (Mark all that apply.)
1. History of falls (2 or more falls - or any fall with an injury - - in the past 12 months)
2. Unintentional weight loss of a total of 10 pounds or more in the past 12 months
3. Multiple hospitalizations (2 or more) in the past 6 months
4. Multiple emergency department visits (2 or more) in the past 6 months
5. Decline in mental, emotional, or behavioral status in the past 3 months
6. Reported or observed history of difficulty complying with any medical instructions (for example, medications, diet, exercise) in the past 3 months
Prior history of falls within 3 months:
An unintentional change in position resulting in coming to rest on the ground or at a lower level
Inability to make it to the bathroom or commode in timely manner.
Includes but not limited to, macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathies, visual field loss, age related changes, decline in visual acuity, accommodation, glare tol
erance, depth perception, and night vision or not wearing prescribed glasses or having the correct prescription.
May include patients who need help with IADLS or ADLS or have gait or transfer problems, arthritis, pain, fear of falling, foot problems, impaired sensation, imp
aired coordination or improper use of assistive devices.
May include but not limited to, poor illumination, equipment tubing, inappropriate footwear, pets, hard to reach items, floor surfaces that are uneven or cluttered,
Poly Pharmacy (4 or more prescriptions-any type):
All PRESCRIPTIONS including prescriptions for OTC meds. Drugs highly associated with fall risk include but not limited to, sedatives, antidepressants, tranquili
zers, narcotics, antihypertensives, cardiac meds, corticosteroids, anti-anxiety drugs, anticholinergic drugs, and hypoglycemic drugs.
Pain often affects an individual's desire or ability to move or pain can be a factor in depression or compliance with safety recommendations.
Could include patients with dementia, Alzheimer's or stroke patients or patients who are confused, use poor judgment, have decreased comprehension, impulsi
vity, memory deficits. Consider patients ability to adhere to the plan of care.

A. Self Care: Code the patient's need for assistance with bathing, dressing, using the toilet, or

B. Indoor Mobility (Ambulation): Code the patient's need for assistance with walking from room

Indicate devices and aids used by the patient prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injury (Check all that apply)
Code the patient's usual performance at SOC/ROC for each activity using the 6-point scale. If activity was not attempted at SOC/ROC, code the reason.
Safety and Quality of Performance - If helper assistance is required because patient's performance is unsafe or of poor quality, score according to amount of assistance provided.
Activities may be completed with or without assistive devices.
06. Independent - Patient completes the activity by him/herself with no assistance from a helper.
05. Setup or clean-up assistance - Helper sets up or cleans up; patient completes activity. Helper assists only prior to or following the activity.
Helper provides verbal cues and/or touching/steadying and/or contact guard assistance as patient completes activity. Assistance may be provided throughout the activity or intermittently.
03. Partial/moderate assistance - Helper does LESS THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts, holds or supports trunk or limbs, but provides less than half the effort.
02. Substantial/maximal assistance - Helper does MORE THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts or holds trunk or limbs and provides more than half the effort.
01. Dependent - Helper does ALL of the effort. Patient does none of the effort to complete the activity. Or, the assistance of 2 or more helpers is required for the patient to complete the activity.
If activity was not attempted, code reason:
09. Not applicable - Not attempted and the patient did not perform this activity prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.
10. Not attempted due to environmental limitations (e.g., lack of equipment, weather constraints)

Current Ability to Dress Upper Body safely (with or without dressing aids) including undergarments, pullovers, front-opening shirts and blouses, managing zippers, buttons, and snaps:

2. Someone must help the patient put on upper body clothing.
3. Patient depends entirely upon another person to dress the upper body.
Current Ability to Dress Lower Body safely (with or without dressing aids) including undergarments, slacks, socks or nylons, shoes:
0. Able to obtain, put on, and remove clothing and shoes without assistance.

the shower or tub, OR (c) for washing difficult to reach areas.

FUNCTIONAL ASSESSMENT (GAIT AND RANGE OF MOTION)
PLAN OF CARE ORDERS AND DISCHARGE PLAN
Orders and Treatment (Specify Frequency, Duration and Purpose of Visits): $484.60 (2.iv)
SN Frequency: 2/wk X wk2, 1/wk X wk2
PT Frequency: 1/wk X wk1, 2/wk X wk3
ADMIT PATIENT TO ACTIVE HOME HEALTH SERVICES. ALL DIAGNOSIS AND MEDICATIONS VERIFIED WITH THE PRIMARY CARE PHYSICIAN.
The patient is considered homebound because he has severe left knee medial compartment osteoarthritis with a flexible 5° varus deformity and medial joint-line tenderness that produces
sharp pain with activity and aching at rest, and he cannot ambulate more than 200 meters at a time due to knee pain. Exam findings document limited functional mobility and pain limiting
therapeutic exercise tolerance; the patient reports difficulty ascending and descending stairs and requires assistive-device training and home safety/fall-precaution interventions (physical
therapy was ordered for gait/balance/transfer training, assistive device education, ROM and therapeutic strengthening, and post-op exercise preparation). Leaving the home requires a
considerable and taxing effort because even short bouts of ambulation provoke severe pain, impair safe stair negotiation, and necessitate assistance and optimization of transfers and
assistive-device use. Allowable absences should be limited and infrequent restricted to medically necessary appointments or essential, short, supervised trips only. These objectives,
documented functional impairments and planned skilled interventions (PT for gait/transfer training, therapeutic exercise, assistive-device education, and home-safety/fall precautions)
demonstrate that the patient meets Medicare homebound criteria and that skilled home health services are medically necessary to enable safe mobility, prevent falls, and prepare for the
HH SN frequency: 2 times a week for 2 weeks, 1 time a week for 2 weeks for skilled observation and assessment, medication reconciliation, pain assessment, wound monitoring and
management, infection control measures, diet, home safety and disease process education.
HH PT frequency: 1 time a week for 1 week, 2 times a week /for weeks for evaluation and treatment for mobility, gait, transfer, establish therapeutic exercises, strengthening, home safety and
fall prevention techniques training effective on 04/04/2026.
-Patient will ambulate 300 meters on level surfaces with an assistive device and pain ≤4/10 to improve community mobility tolerance within 4 weeks.
-Patient will ascend and descend 4 steps with one handrail and an assistive device safely, demonstrating improved balance and reduced fall risk within 4 weeks.
-Patient will perform sit-to-stand and bed-to-chair transfers independently with proper technique and safe use of assistive device within 4 weeks.
-Patient will complete 15 minutes of therapeutic exercise (ROM and strengthening) with pain ≤3/10 to enhance pre-operative conditioning within 4 weeks.
-Patient will verbalize and demonstrate 3 fall-prevention strategies and safe assistive-device use during home mobility tasks within 4 weeks.
Start of care, plan of care, patient's status and frequencies.
Start of care, plan of care, patient's status and frequencies.
Will continue to assess and evaluate patient understanding, functional status, and knowledge of self-care management on the next visit.

Timothy Boyer is a 70-year-old male patient who has been referred for home health care services on March 25, 2026. The attending physician overseeing his care is Dr. Ronald Hillock. The
discipline completing the assessment is Physical Therapy, with the start of care date set for April 4, 2026. It is noteworthy that Timothy was not discharged from any inpatient facility within the
past 14 days, indicating that he has transitioned directly to home health services from his prior living situation.
Timothy has been assessed as homebound based on specific criteria. He is unable to leave his home due to illness and injury following his recent joint replacement surgery, which has made
ambulation challenging. He experiences significant weakness and pain, which restricts his mobility. Additionally, he has balance issues that increase his risk of falls, requiring him to use walls
and furniture for support when moving. Leaving home is an exhausting task that necessitates extended rest periods for recovery, as Timothy experiences severe pain during ambulation. This
assessment categorizes his homebound status as medically contraindicated for leaving home.
The primary reason for Timothy's need for home health services is his aftercare following joint replacement surgery (diagnosis code Z47.1). This surgical intervention necessitates rehabilitation
support to ensure proper recovery and rehabilitation. His primary diagnosis is further supported by the presence of a left artificial knee joint (diagnosis code Z96.652) and a history of falling
(diagnosis code Z91.81). Notably, there are no additional comorbidities or co-existing conditions reported.
In conducting a head-to-toe assessment, Timothy's cardiopulmonary function appears stable; his vital signs reveal a temperature of 98.6°F, a pulse rate of 82 beats per minute, blood pressure
of 115/72 mmHg, and a respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute. Gastrointestinal assessment indicates no abnormalities. The integumentary system shows a surgical wound that is not
observable due to a non-removable dressing. Safety assessments indicate that Timothy is at risk for falls, particularly due to his impaired balance and mobility.
Psychosocially, Timothy is oriented and alert, exhibiting no acute changes in mental status. His mood interview indicates a total severity score of 0 on the PHQ-2 assessment, suggesting he
rarely feels lonely or isolated. He lives with another person who assists him with daily activities, ensuring he has some level of social support.
Timothy's cognitive function is intact, as evidenced by his performance on the Brief Interview for Mental Status (BIMS) where he scored 15. He shows no signs of confusion or disorganized
thinking and is capable of following directions and recalling information. Despite expressing feelings of mild depression, he does not exhibit any suicidal or homicidal ideation.
In terms of activities of daily living (ADLs), Timothy requires assistance with grooming and bathing. He can dress himself if clothing is handed to him but needs supervision for toileting and
bathing. His mobility is significantly affected; he uses a walker for ambulation and requires human assistance to negotiate stairs or uneven surfaces. His functional ability is categorized as
independent in self-care, but he needs setup assistance for eating, oral hygiene, and dressing.
Timothy has a nutritional health screen score of 5, indicating good nutritional status. He is currently taking five or more medications, which places him at risk for potential adverse effects. These
medications include opioids and antiplatelet drugs, which require careful management and education regarding high-risk medication use. There have been no reports of urinary incontinence,
but he experiences very rare bowel incontinence.
Timothy's primary caregiver is a non-agency individual who resides with him, providing essential support for his daily needs. The caregiver is involved in assisting with medications and daily
activities, ensuring Timothy's safety and well-being.
In conclusion, Timothy Boyer demonstrates a clear medical necessity for skilled services in home health care due to his recent joint replacement surgery and the associated functional
limitations. His care plan will include physical therapy to enhance his mobility and strength, along with ongoing education regarding medication management and safety measures to prevent
falls. Recommended visit frequency for Timothy will be determined based on his progress and needs, ensuring he receives the appropriate level of care to facilitate his recovery and
CARE PLAN (PROBLEMS I GOALS I INTERVENTIONS)
Patient Care Plan / Goals I Interventions:
Patient's vital signs will be within normal limits.
Check O2 saturation every visit for any shortness of breath on exertion, fatigue/weakness. Check VS every visit and report to PMD if patient vitals are not within the parameters.
Demonstrate pursed-lip breathing exercises, deep breathing exercises, and energy-conserving measures.
Review patient/caregiver on methods to conserve energy, frequent rest periods, sitting instead of standing to perform tasks, and performing slower, purposeful movements which will
limit fatigue. Instruct patient/caregiver on pursed lip breathing exercises, deep breathing exercises to alleviate dyspnea.
Patient will be free from signs/symptoms of cardiac and respiratory distress during the certification period. Verbalize understanding of symptoms of cardiac complications and when to call 911.
Reinforce patient/caregiver on sodium limitations, managing stress, avoiding smoking/alcohol intake, and compliance with prescribed medications to control blood pressure. Encourage
the patient/caregiver to provide a quiet environment, limiting stimuli to promote rest. Advise the patient to get up slowly to offset the feeling of dizziness. Instruct patient/caregiver to notify
SN/MD for signs/symptoms of cardiac and respiratory distress like chest pain, cyanosis, dependent edema, jugular vein distention, palpitations, wheezing, increased respiratory rate, anxiety,
headache, lightheadedness, and shortness of breath.
Patient will be free from cardiopulmonary distress during the care episode. Patient's pulse and blood pressure will be within established parameters.
Reinforce patient/caregiver to check and record blood pressure, including heart rate. Assess cardiopulmonary status every visit.
Problem: Aftercare following joint replacement surgery
Achieve pain control and manage post-operative symptoms.
Assess pain level and adjust pain management plan as needed.
Educate patient and family on pain management techniques, including medication adherence.
Develop a tailored physical therapy exercise program to improve strength and mobility.
Educate patient and family on safe mobility techniques to prevent falls.
Perform regular assessments of functional capabilities and adjust therapy as needed.
Problem: Presence of left artificial knee joint
Improve mobility and increase strength in the left leg
Implement a physical therapy program focusing on strengthening exercises for the left leg and promoting safe ambulation.
Assist with gait training to improve balance and mobility using assistive devices as needed.
Educate the patient and family on exercises that can be done at home to support mobility.
Manage pain associated with the artificial knee joint
Assess pain levels and develop a pain management plan that may include medication management.
Educate the patient and family on pain management techniques, including the use of heat and cold therapy.
Reduce the frequency of falls by 50% within 3 months.
Conduct a comprehensive fall risk assessment to identify environmental hazards.
Implement a personalized home exercise program to improve strength and balance.
Encourage the use of assistive devices, if needed, and provide training on their proper use.
Increase the patient's confidence in mobility and reduce fear of falling.
Engage the patient in mobility training sessions to enhance their confidence.
Provide education on the importance of regular physical activity to build strength and confidence.
Verbalize understanding of the importance of pressure-relieving measures.
Instruct patient/caregiver on pressure-relieving measures such as repositioning every 2 hours, float heels, pad bony prominences and methods to reduce friction and shear.
Instruct patient/caregiver to inspect skin daily and notify SN/MD for redness or open sores. Assess skin for breakdown due to risk of pressure ulcer development.
Problem: Rehospitalization Risk and Medication Management
Patient will be free from side effects and adverse medication reactions during the entire episode. Patient/Caregiver will demonstrate medication safety and compliance. Patients will have no re-
Identify Medication Interactions and evaluate patient/family/caregiver(s) understanding of medication compliance.
